solutions.solution: Since A is telling B about Madhu in a matter-of-fact tone, this much is the statement that Sunil can know Madhu. But it is not certain whether he inquired and Sunil can give. Therefore, I is not implicit. this acquaintance is so factual and formal that it is just that the person is stating the whole logic on the basis of which he infers that the machine will reach the conclusion, Madhu has gone mad.

When the economists say that prices should fall after May because the demand starts dropping. It appears as if demand remains heavy till then and so, in the short run, prices might not drop before May. So, I is implicit. Similarly, III has nothing to do with the statement. Similarly, III has nothing to do with the statement, although it seems right.
If a divorce the basis of which the CM is demanding more funds, I may or may not be true enough indications are not given in the CM's statements. The same is true of III as well.

It is implicit because A cannot give wedding gifts without being invited to weddings. It is implicit because A wouldn't have raised the point of "consumer goods" as wedding gifts. III is not implicit because A never mentions consumer goods as 'useless' gifts.
The newspaper must have had authentic and reliable sources to publish such news but it is nowhere mentioned that it has quoted such sources. Hence I is not implicit but II is obvious.

The teacher does not mean that the students are not intelligent. He only says that questions are difficult and need additional strategy. I Hence, I is not implicit but III is. II is not implicit; in fact, the teacher says that the problem with these students is that they don't have strategy.
I and III are obvious. But "intoxicating effect" of whisky has never been mentioned.

I is obvious by the way the two sentences have been put together. II is obvious that A thought that people's view about coffee (that it was bad for health) was not correct, why would he decide to cut down on it? But Brandy is not the issue. A state of feeling action: III may or may not follow. It is not sure whether B drinks coffee at all.

I is obvious. But the very fact that there are some persons who want to buy used imported cars, III is obvious because bad there been no such people willing to afford such items, the advertisers wouldn't have placed advertisements for used cars. Whenever something is 'meant', Remember this rule)

To get the assumptions, try to find what the politician wants us to think. He wants us to think that the charges are false (and not true, although beyond proof). Hence, I is implicit while II is not. The politician is not fed up with people; he seems fed up of the charges levelled against him.
I is obviously rubbish; the statement uses the term "history" not in the sense of a subject but in the general sense. II is obvious by the second sentence. III is just a matter-of-fact statement.

People returning to Supreme Court means they are weary of an inactive executive. But it is never implied that the Court is more active than the executive. But it is never implied that the Supreme Court is more powerful or that the executive does not have adequate powers. The question of power has never been touched in the statement.

The author is not against adult franchise in general. He says adult franchise if III is practised by illiterate people. In other words, he is against universal adult franchise. Hence, I is not implicit. II is obviously implicit. III, again, is an assumption for obvious reasons. (See, Section 3.6, (v)).
I is obvious in fact, it is taken as a universal truth. II is obvious because the mother forbids sweets as they are not "good for teeth". III is obvious by the tone of the author's language.

I is obvious in the advertisement. II is obvious. (Whenever an advertisement highlights any aspect of a product, it is assumed that people accept that aspect. Remember that rule.) The ad does not suggest that price is of secondary importance to people because otherwise they would have highlighted the cheapness of the product and not its quality. So, III is implicit.

The mother is concerned because the noise may hamper her son's study. Hence I is implicit. But there is nothing which can suggest that noise is not conducive to children in general. All that we can infer from the statement is that noise is not conducive to their study; that's all. Also, we may not be able to conclude from the statement that the mother cares for her child's health; in the statement the mother has shown concern not for her child's health, but for study.
I is obvious. But there is nothing to suggest that hockey is a different game or that chances of victory are different from actual victories. So, II and III are not implied.

The thief uses the gun to arouse fear. This fear brings a sense of command or compulsion. Hence II is implicit. But I is not. The thief uses the phrase "behave like a good boy" in a tone of sarcasm only; we should not take it in a literal sense. Similarly, III is also not implicit.
The word "unless" implies that presently there is neither autonomy nor profit in the units. Hence, I is implicit. II and III are obvious.
